summary: I currently live in Boise, Idaho, and it's where I base much of my music and teaching work. During this interview in May 2021, I was speaking from Boise while the interviewer was in Twin Falls. Boise feels like home and a place where I've been able to sustain my career as a musician and dance teacher. It's also where I balance my creative work with some medical assistant duties during slower months.

The interview for this oral history took place with me in Boise and the interviewer in Twin Falls, Idaho. While I don't live there, Twin Falls is part of the regional context for this conversation. It represents the broader Idaho area where my music and community connections exist. The geographic distance between Boise and Twin Falls highlights how technology allowed us to connect during the pandemic.

I was born in Logan, Utah, on February 2, 1987. Logan is where my life began before I eventually moved to Idaho. Although I don't live there now, it remains a part of my personal history and identity. My upbringing and family background trace back to places like Logan, shaping who I am today.

Culturally, I was raised in the Latter-day Saints community, which influenced my early life and identity. However, I no longer identify as a Christian or Mormon; my beliefs have evolved toward paganism and humanist atheism. The LDS background is part of my cultural heritage but not my current religious identity. This shift reflects my personal journey and how I relate to spirituality today.

Tambalka is one of my musical projects where I perform original music in a syllabic language. It's not scat singing or improvisation but carefully crafted melodies sung as if the human voice were an instrument. This project reflects my deep interest in treating the voice like any other instrument, a perspective I developed especially after working with my mentor Jeff Baker. Tambalka represents a unique blend of my vocal skills and creative expression.

Jeff Baker is one of my mentors who profoundly influenced my approach to singing. He was the first to treat my voice like an instrument, running scales and teaching improvisation and modes. This kind of training was something I hadn't experienced before, especially outside of classical settings. Jeff's guidance helped me break stereotypes about vocalists and empowered me to hold my own in band settings.

While I didn't mention Treefort Music Festival explicitly in this portion of the interview, it is part of the local music scene in Boise where I perform and connect with other musicians. Events like Treefort are important for community building and opportunities for artists like me. I had hoped to be involved in festival seasons in 2020 before the pandemic disrupted those plans. Festivals remain a vital part of my professional landscape.
